evidence: uploadsvc is reusing protocol primitives (`Action`, `UnsupportedActionError`) instead of defining a stable service-local command contract.
Upload action support is declared in `actions.go` (`uploadSupported`) and separately in `service.go` (`actionBindings`), creating two coupled sources of supported-action truth.
The adapter test file also imports protocol action constants directly, amplifying cross-module coupling at the test boundary.
In `SupportHandlerWithRuntime`, request parsing, workspace lifecycle, artifact collection, packaging, external submission, and transition publishing are all in a single handler-path flow.

`importer.go` embeds upload transport + chunk persistence logic and also controls imported-pier business outcomes (`configureUploadedPier`, `runImportedPierWorkflowFn`, and post-import workflow calls) inside the same pipeline type.

Docker restart/recovery policy is coupled to event handling in `routines/docker.go` (auto-restart path in `dockerStopTransition`, delayed restarts in `scheduleShipRestart`, and retry behavior in `check502Loop`) rather than isolated into reusable policy objects.
`collectUrbitViewInputs` in `collectors.go` performs remote config resolution, orchestration queries, and status shaping in one function before a simple mapping step.

goseg/internal/seams/runtime.go panics on unconfigured seams in both `panicOnNilSeamFn` and every `RequireSeam*` variant, converting configuration/setup issues into process panics instead of returned errors.
The seam default/fallback helpers (`DefaultIfNil*` and `RequireSeam*`) have mixed failure semantics: callers can trigger hard-fail panics instead of recoverable `error` returns.
goseg/system/wifi.go uses `fmt.Errorf("%w; restore C2C mode after failed connect: %v", connectErr, c2cErr)` which preserves only the first error in the chain and formats the second as text.
goseg/importer/importer.go uses `fmt.Errorf("%w: %v", errImportPierConfig, err)` in `finalizeUploadOnCompletion`, similarly dropping a typed secondary error from chain semantics.

findings.summary: Upload service exposes protocol action/error types, leaking transport contracts across module boundaries
evidence: `goseg/uploadsvc/service.go` defines `type Action = actions.Action` and `type UnsupportedActionError = actions.UnsupportedActionError`, so callers of uploadsvc inherit protocol package types.
This couples upload service API evolution directly to protocol package edits instead of via a service-owned boundary contract.
findings.suggestion: Introduce uploadsvc-owned exported action/error types and adapt them at the adapter edge (e.g., translate `actions.Action` to `uploadsvc.Action` in one adapter translator), then remove direct alias/export coupling from `service.go`.

findings.summary: Supported upload actions are duplicated between protocol and service modules, risking drift
evidence: `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` maintains `uploadSupported` for parse-time validation.
`goseg/uploadsvc/service.go` separately encodes supported runtime actions through `actionBindings` keys.
A new/removed action must be kept in sync across both lists manually; no shared canonical source is enforced here.
findings.suggestion: Derive both validation and dispatch bindings from a single uploadsvc-owned definition (e.g., local action descriptor registry used to build the parser whitelist and dispatcher map), then keep protocol package generic and transport-agnostic.

findings.summary: Upload action governance has two independent canonical lists, creating unavoidable drift risk across protocol parsing and dispatch execution.
evidence: `protocol/actions/actions.go` defines `uploadSupported = []Action{ActionUploadOpenEndpoint, ActionUploadReset}` for parser validation.
`goseg/uploadsvc/service.go` separately encodes supported/handled actions in `actionBindings` with handlers for the same action names.

Tests in `goseg/uploadsvc/adapters/upload_payload_test.go` directly reference action constants from `actions` while also validating upload payload dispatch behavior, coupling behavior expectations to two independent sources.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single upload action contract definition in `goseg/protocol/actions` (including handler metadata where needed) and generate or derive both parser validation and service dispatch bindings from it, so adding/removing actions is impossible to do in one layer without failing compile/runtime gates in the other.

findings.summary: Reset command contracts are over-coupled to an endpoint-centric request shape, weakening transport contract boundaries and allowing unrelated payload fields to flow into reset paths.
evidence: `Command` uses a single `OpenEndpointRequest` payload for all upload actions, including `ActionUploadReset`.

The reset binding in `actionBindings` ignores request content and still executes `service.Reset()`, yet tests expect request fields to be parsed and present for reset (`reset-with-extra-fields` case).
Action parsing in `protocol/actions` validates only the action token, with no per-action payload contract enforcement here, so reset and open-endpoint payloads share a structurally broad envelope.
findings.suggestion: Refactor upload command modeling to be action-specific (e.g., `type Command struct { Action Action; OpenEndpointRequest *OpenEndpointRequest; ResetRequest *ResetRequest }` or a typed union) and validate/purge payload fields per action at adapter boundary so each namespace action owns a strict contract.

findings.summary: Support request handler mixes API handling, artifact orchestration, and policy-driven event publishing in one flow.
evidence: `SupportHandlerWithRuntime` decodes JSON (`decodeSupportRequest`), prepares filesystem artifacts, captures optional CPU profile, creates zip output, sends HTTP report, and publishes system transitions.
Failure/success outcomes are handled inline via local state (`supportHandlerOutcome`) and in-function publish hooks, instead of through a dedicated application service boundary.
findings.suggestion: Split this handler into separate layers: request adapter, support artifact service, submission client, and transition publisher; make each component independently testable and composable.

findings.summary: Upload pipeline couples multipart transport mechanics with imported-pier domain orchestration and deployment side effects.
evidence: The `importUploadPipeline` type and `run()` method own both transport/session state concerns and post-upload side effects.

Completion step `setUploadFinalizationState` calls `finalizeUploadOnCompletion`, which directly combines chunks and invokes `configureUploadedPier` (which triggers `runImportedPierWorkflowFn` + `runImportedPierPostImportFn`).
This makes lower-level upload handling responsible for deployment decisions and post-processing cleanup behavior.
findings.suggestion: Introduce an explicit `UploadImportCoordinator` that emits a validated import command and delegates orchestration of imported-pier workflows to a separate ship-import service.

findings.summary: Broadcast collector function acts as a broad composition hub instead of a narrow projection builder.
evidence: `collectUrbitViewInputs` loads urbit config per ship, queries orchestration/container state, consults startram readiness, and builds remote/local UI payload fields in one step.
`ConstructPierInfo` chains `runtimeSnapshotForPiers`, `collectUrbitViewInputs`, and mapper assembly with broad module touchpoints (`config`, `orchestration`, `startram`, `system`, `click`).
findings.suggestion: Split collector responsibilities into data fetchers (snapshot providers per domain) and a thin composer that only maps domain DTOs into broadcast view models; cache expensive dependencies where applicable.

findings.summary: Docker lifecycle recovery policy is hard-coded in event flow code, increasing coupling and reducing policy reuse.
evidence: `dockerStopTransition` enforces restart decisions directly (`startContainerFn`) when desired state is not stopped.
`dockerDieAfterTransition` conditionally schedules background restarts with fixed `2 * time.Second` delay in `scheduleShipRestart`.
`check502Loop` contains fixed polling/retry timing (`180s`, `120s`) and direct recovery delegation, duplicating recovery cadence with other restart paths.
findings.suggestion: Externalize restart/recovery policy into a typed policy provider (durations, strike thresholds, fallback actions) and inject it into the transition workflow so behavior can be reasoned about and centrally tuned.

findings.summary: Nil runtime-seam configuration uses panics instead of error returns, so missing seams crash the process instead of surfacing recoverable configuration errors.
evidence: runtime seam helpers include `panic(fmt.Sprintf("runtime seam function %s is not configured", name))` in `panicOnNilSeamFn`.
`DefaultIfNil*` and `RequireSeam*` in the same file return `noop`/panic closures for missing dependencies instead of returning an `error`.
Error flow in operations modules is otherwise built from error-returning helpers; introducing panics at seam boundaries bypasses standard error propagation and recovery.
findings.suggestion: Replace nil-seam behavior with explicit error-returning functions and fail-fast initialization checks (or constructor-time validation) that return a descriptive error instead of panicking at call time; keep panics only for truly unrecoverable invariants.

findings.summary: Some composite error constructions only preserve one cause, turning multi-failure paths into single-error surfaces and preventing robust caller-side classification.
evidence: In `system/wifi.go`, `C2CConnect` returns `fmt.Errorf("%w; restore C2C mode after failed connect: %v", connectErr, c2cErr)` which stringifies `c2cErr` instead of wrapping it.
In `importer/importer.go`, `finalizeUploadOnCompletion` uses `fmt.Errorf("%w: %v", errImportPierConfig, err)` and also loses typed access to the second error.
findings.suggestion: Use multi-cause wrapping (`fmt.Errorf` with multiple `%w`, or `errors.Join`) for any branch that should report both primary and cleanup/fallback failures so callers can `errors.Is`/`errors.As` against both causes.
